questions.questionText: Cherry-red spot on macula is seen in:
questions.optionB: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO — sudden painless loss of vision; fovea appears red against pale ischemic retina due to its choroidal blood supply)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Cherry-red spot = CRAO (pale retina + red fovea — fovea has choroidal supply). Why correct: CRAO: embolus blocks central retinal artery → retinal ischemia → pale/edematous retina → fovea appears cherry-red (thin fovea reveals underlying choroidal circulation). Other causes: Tay-Sachs disease, Niemann-Pick disease (lipid storage diseases — gangliosides accumulate in retinal ganglion cells, spare fovea). Why A wrong: DR — microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton wool spots, neovascularization. Why C wrong: CRVO — 'blood and thunder' fundus (widespread hemorrhages, dilated tortuous veins, disc edema). Why D wrong: Optic neuritis — disc edema, afferent pupillary defect, painful eye movements (MS association). Clinical relevance: CRAO = ophthalmologic emergency (<4-6 hours to restore flow). Treatment: ocular massage, anterior chamber paracentesis, carbogen inhalation. Exam trap: Cherry-red spot at macula differentials: CRAO (acute), Tay-Sachs, Niemann-Pick, Sandhoff disease. Memory aid: 'CRAO = Cherry-Red Against pale retina + Acute vision loss.' Reference: Khurana – Retina.
questions.conceptTag: CRAO cherry-red spot macula sudden painless blindness
